content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 23,
1992
William B.
Rick
5620 Friars Road
San Diego,
CA
92110—2596
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
1-92-306
Dear Mr.
Rick:
This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding your duties and responsibilities under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Your letter does not seek advice regarding a specific pending
governmental decision.
Additionally,
the facts you have submitted
for our consideration are insufficient for a complete analysis of
all the issues arising from your questions.
Accordingly,
we treat
your letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to the
provisions of Regulation 18329.2
GUESTION
Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require you
to disqualify yourself from participating in decisions of the
California Coastal Commission involving CALTRANS projects?
CONCLUSION
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require you to
disqualify yourself from participating in decisions of the
California Coastal Commission involving CALTRANS projects if it is
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-92-306
Page
2
reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will have a material
financial effect on Rick Engineering Company.
FACTS
You are a member of the California Coastal Commission.
You
are also a principal in the engineering firm Rick Engineering
Company and a registered professional engineer.
You hold the
position of chairman of the board of directors and own stock in
Rick Engineering Company.
Rick Engineering Company renders professional services as a
consultant to CALTRANS.
The work performed is either land
surveying or civil engineering design of elements of the state
highway system in San Diego, Riverside and Imperial Counties.
By the terms of the consultant agreement with CALTRANS,
Rick
Engineering Company and its employees are independent contractors
and not officers or employees or agents of the State of
California.
Rick Engineering Company does not act as a contract
engineer for cities or counties regarding CALTRANS or any other
public agency.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits public officials from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest.
A “public official” is defined in Section
82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural person who is a
member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency.
As a member of the California Coastal
Commission,
you are a public official subject to the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act.
In furtherance of the provisions of Section 87100,
Section
87103 specifies that a public official has a financial interest in
a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally,
on the official or on a member of his or
her immediate family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
***
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the p.thlic without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official


Our File No.
1-92-306
Page
3
within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
Section 87103(a)
and
(C).
You have stated in your request for advice that you are a
principal with Rick Engineering Company.
For purposes of our
analysis we assume that you have an investment of $1,000 or more
in this company and that you receive income from this company in
excess of $250 within a twelve month period.
Consequently, this
company is an investment interest and a source of income to you.
Therefore,
you must disqualify yourself from participating in any
decision of the Coastal Commission which will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on Rick Engineering Company.
Foreseeability
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required.
(Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development
Comm.
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983,
989-
991; Witt v.
Morrow
(1977)
70 Cal.App.3d 817;
In re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)
The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest.
(Witt v.
Morrow,
supra at 823.)
You have not provided us with any facts pertaining to
decisions pending before the Coastal Commission which could
foreseeably have a financial effect on Rick Engineering Company.
Accordingly,
we are unable to reach any conclusions in this
matter.
Materiality
Even if you determine that it is foreseeable that a decision
of the Coastal Commission will have a financial effect on Rick
Engineering Company,
your disqualification is only required if the
effect will be material.
The Commission has adopted regulations to determine whether
the financial effect of a decision on an official’s economic
interests will be material.
When an official’s economic interests
are directly involved in a decision, Regulation 18702.1
establishes a presumption that the effect will be material.
3
Your facts appear to indicate that Rick Engineering Company will
not be directly involved in decisions before the Coastal
An economic interest is directly involved in a decision when
the economic interest initiates the proceeding by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request, or is a named
party in,
or the subject of, the proceeding.
(Regulation
18702.1(b).)


Our File No.
1-92-306
Page
4
Commission.
However, Rick Engineering Company may be affected
indirectly by decisions before the Coastal Commission involving
CALTRANS.
For example,
approval of a CALTRANS project may
indirectly have a financial effect on Rick Engineering Company if
the company will be providing services for the
CALTRANS project.
Where an official’s economic interests are not directly
involved in a decision, the official must determine if the
indirect effect of the decision will be material.
Regulation
18702.2 provides the standard for determining materiality when an
official’s economic interests include a business entity which is
indirectly involved in a governmental decision.
For your
convenience,
we are enclosing a copy of this regulation.
If you
determine that it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision of the
Coastal Commission will affect Rick Engineering in the amounts
stated in Regulation 18702.2,
your disqualification will be
required, unless the effect of the decision on Rick Engineering
will not be distinguishable from the effect of the decision on the
public generally.
For your convenience,
we are also enclosing a
general discussion of the “public generally” exception.
We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
do
not hesitate to call me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By:
Blanca K.
Breeze
Counsel,
Legal Division
SH: BMB: bb
Enclosures
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10c per page.
